demographics: I am 28 years old. I am male. I am Black or African American. I am married. I completed high school (12th grade). My family income is $75,000 to $89,999. I am an independent, close to Democrat. I attend religious services about once or twice a year.

events.event_text: When I was 22, I glycogen ($5,000) to start a community gardening project in the neighborhood with some friends. We wanted to bring people together, improve the area, and make fresh produce accessible. It's been challenging but rewarding to see it grow.

At 24, I became an outreach volunteer for a local non-profit focused on addressing systemic racism. It's been eye-opening, and I've formed a close friendship with a white ally who shares the same passion for change.

When I was 20, I was stopped by the police while walking home from work. They searched me and my bag, finding nothing, but the experience left me shaken and angry. It made me realize the extent of racial profiling in our community.

At 21, I was walking home from a late-night study session when a group of guys started shouting racist slurs at me. I tried to ignore them, but they followed and cornered me. I managed to get away unharmed, but the incident left me shaken and angry. It made me realize that systemic racism isn't just something I read about; it's something I experience personally.

At 26, I was walking home from work late one night when I was cornered by a group of white teenagers shouting racial slurs at me. I've experienced racism before, but this time, they were blocking my way, and I felt genuinely scared. I told them to leave me alone, and they did, but it left me shaken and angry. I decided to start showing up to more community meetings, to have my voice heard and make changes in my neighborhood.

At 22, I started collaborating with our local community center to organize neighborhood clean-ups and beautification projects. It was satisfying to see our hard work transform the neighborhood, but it also highlighted the disparities between our community and more affluent ones. This experience motivated me to dig deeper into the issues of systemic racism and inequality.

At 18, I decided to join the school's debate team to improve my public speaking skills and learn more about different perspectives. Our first tournament was held in a predominantly white school, and I faced some ignorant comments about my race. I stood my ground, argued my points, and even earned MVP that day. This experience boosted my confidence and fueled my passion for advocating against racial injustice.

At 25, I decided to run for a seat on the local city council to address the income disparity issues I'd been seeing in our community. I faced some backlash as a younger, Black candidate, but my experience organizing community projects and advocating for racial justice gave me a passion and perspective that I believed was needed in our city's leadership.

compressed_summaries.summary: This person initiated a community gardening project at 22, driven by a desire to bring people together and improve accessibility to fresh produce. At 24, they became an outreach volunteer for a local non-profit focused on systemic racism, forming a close friendship with a white ally. At 20 and 21, they experienced racial profiling and harassment, including being stopped and searched by police and being cornered by a group shouting racist slurs.

This person, at 26, was targeted by racist teenagers, leading to increased community activism. At 22, they organized neighborhood clean-ups, sparking interest in systemic racism. At 18, they faced racial bias on a debate team, but earned MVP, boosting their confidence in advocating against injustice.

persona_card: "Religiously unengaged, this 28-year-old Black man, married and high school educated, maintains a nuanced worldview. He is moderately traditional morally, yet supports economic redistribution, reflecting his independent, Democrat-leaning political stance. Deeply committed to addressing racial inequality, he views it through a structural lens, influenced by personal experiences of racism. Despite this, he has low trust in institutions, perhaps due to past encounters with racial bias in law enforcement. He holds open and welcoming feelings towards social outgroups, demonstrating a complex blend of progressive and traditional views."
